title: Linux & The Command Line
subtitle: Essential commands, the file system, processes, and SSH — every dev's second home

sections.title: The File System
sections.text:
Most servers run Linux. You don't need to be an expert, but you need to be comfortable in the terminal. Everything starts at / (root):

  • /bin — Essential programs (ls, cp, mv).
  • /etc — Configuration files (nginx.conf, ssh config).
  • /var — Variable data (logs in /var/log).
  • /home — User folders. /home/deploy for your app.
  • /tmp — Temporary files. Wiped on reboot.

Navigate with cd, list files with ls, view files with cat or less. Follow logs with tail -f. That covers 80% of your daily work.

config.caption: Linux file system hierarchy — everything starts from root (/)

sections.caption: The Linux file system: everything branches from root (/)
sections.code:
# Essential navigation
$ pwd              # print working directory
$ ls -la           # list all files with details
$ cd /var/log      # change directory
$ mkdir project    # create directory
$ rm -rf temp/     # remove directory recursively

# File operations
$ cp file.txt backup/
$ mv file.txt newname.txt
$ cat file.txt     # view file contents
$ less file.txt    # scroll through file
$ tail -f log.txt  # follow log file (live)
sections.callout: Learn these commands by heart: ls, cd, cat, grep, ps, top, find, chmod, ssh, curl. They cover 90% of daily terminal work.

sections.title: Process Management
sections.text:
Every running program is a process. You can inspect and control them from the terminal:

  • ps aux — See every process, its user, CPU and memory usage.
  • top or htop — Live process viewer. Press q to quit.
  • kill 1234 — Send SIGTERM (ask nicely). kill -9 1234 — Send SIGKILL (force).
  • systemctl start/stop/status nginx — Manage system services.

Processes run with user permissions. Root (UID 0) can do anything. Regular users can only touch their own files. This is the principle of least privilege — and it's why you don't run your web server as root.

items.heading: ps aux
items.body: Snapshot of all running processes with CPU/memory usage.

items.heading: top/htop
items.body: Real-time process monitor. Press q to quit.

items.heading: kill
items.body: Send signals: SIGTERM (15) = graceful, SIGKILL (9) = force.

items.heading: systemctl
items.body: Manage services: start, stop, restart, enable, status.

items.heading: Permissions
items.body: chmod 755 = rwxr-xr-x. Owner: rwx, group: rx, others: rx.

items.heading: chown
items.body: Change file owner: chown user:group file.txt.

sections.caption: Process management tools every developer should know
sections.code:
$ ps aux | grep nginx
$ top -o %MEM       # sort by memory usage
$ kill -15 1234     # graceful shutdown (SIGTERM)
$ kill -9 1234      # force kill (SIGKILL)

# File permissions
$ chmod 755 script.sh    # rwxr-xr-x
$ chmod +x script.sh     # make executable
$ chown deploy:app config.yml

# Check logs
$ journalctl -u nginx -f  # follow nginx logs

sections.title: SSH — Secure Shell
sections.text:
SSH lets you control a remote server from your terminal. It's how you deploy code, check logs, and fix things in production:

  • ssh user@server — Connect to a server. You'll use this constantly.
  • SSH keys — Generate a key pair with ssh-keygen. Put the public key on the server (ssh-copy-id). Your private key stays secret on your machine. More secure than passwords.
  • scp file.txt user@server:/path/ — Copy files over SSH.
  • ssh -L 5432:localhost:5432 server — Forward a remote port to your local machine. Access the server's database as if it's running locally.

Use ed25519 keys — they're shorter, faster, and more secure than RSA.
sections.caption: SSH creates an encrypted tunnel to a remote server's command line
sections.code:
# Generate SSH key pair
$ ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@email.com"

# Copy public key to server
$ ssh-copy-id user@server-ip

# Connect
$ ssh user@server-ip

# Copy files
$ scp deploy.tar.gz user@server:/var/www/

# Port forwarding (access remote DB locally)
$ ssh -L 5432:localhost:5432 user@server
# Now connect to localhost:5432 to reach the server's PostgreSQL
sections.callout: Use ed25519 keys (they're shorter and more secure than RSA). Never share your private key. Use ssh-agent to avoid typing passphrases repeatedly.
